questions.question: The evidence of the earliest cotton cultivation in the Indus valley is found at which site?
questions.solution:
Early Cotton Cultivation

The earliest evidence of cotton cultivation in the Indian subcontinent comes from Mehrgarh (located in modern-day Pakistan). Archaeologists found traces of cotton preserved in copper beads dating back to approximately 5000 BCE, proving that the region was a pioneer in textile production long before the urban Harappan phase.

questions.question: Dholavira site of Indus Valley Civilization is best known for which of the following practices?
questions.solution:
Water Conservation at Dholavira

Dholavira, located in the Rann of Kutch, Gujarat, is world-renowned for its sophisticated water conservation system. Because the region had low rainfall, the Harappans built massive stone-cut reservoirs and a complex network of channels to store and manage rainwater efficiently.

questions.question: Dholavira is unique for which of the following?
questions.solution:
Uniqueness of Dholavira

Beyond its water systems, Dholavira is unique for being divided into three parts (Citadel, Middle Town, and Lower Town) and for the discovery of a 'Signboard' containing ten large-sized characters of the Harappan script.

questions.question: The Great Bath is located at:
questions.solution:
The Great Bath

The Great Bath is one of the most famous structures of the Indus Valley Civilization, located at Mohenjo-daro. It was a large, rectangular tank made of brick and bitumen, likely used for ritual bathing or purification ceremonies.

questions.question: The Maski Ashoka Inscription is situated in which state of India?
questions.solution:
Maski Inscription Location

The Maski Ashoka Inscription is located in the Raichur district of Karnataka. This site is geographically significant as it marks the southern reach of the Mauryan Empire's direct administrative influence.

questions.question: Which Gumpha at Khandagiri in Odisha, India is known for carvings of twenty-four Jain Teerthankars?
questions.solution:
Trushula Gumpha

At the Khandagiri caves in Odisha, the Trushula Gumpha (Cave 15) is notable for its relief carvings depicting the twenty-four Jain Tirthankaras. These caves, along with Udayagiri, were developed as residential retreats for Jain monks during the reign of King Kharavela.

questions.question: Kanishka, the Kushana ruler, was a patron of which religion?
questions.solution:
Patronage of Kanishka

Kanishka the Great was a devout patron of Mahayana Buddhism. He is famous for convening the Fourth Buddhist Council in Kashmir, which led to the codification of Buddhist scriptures and the spread of the religion to Central Asia and China.

questions.question: Who administered the agrahara lands during the post-Gupta period?
questions.solution:
Agrahara Lands

In the post-Gupta period, Agrahara referred to land grants made specifically to Brahmins or religious institutions. These lands were typically tax-free, and the Brahmin donees were responsible for the administration and upkeep of the area, making them self-governing units.

questions.question: Lothal was important for _______ during the Indus Valley Civilization.
questions.solution:
Maritime Trade at Lothal

Lothal served as a vital maritime trade hub for the Indus Valley Civilization. Its artificial dockyard provided access to the Gulf of Khambhat, facilitating the export of beads, gems, and ivory to Mesopotamia and Egypt.

questions.question: Who among the following issued the earliest land grant of the Maitraka family in 502 CE?
questions.solution:
Maitraka Land Grants

Dronasimha is recorded as the first ruler of the Maitraka dynasty of Vallabhi to issue a land grant in 502 CE. While the dynasty was founded by Bhatarka, Dronasimha was the first to adopt the title of 'Maharaja', indicating growing sovereign power.

questions.question: The royal title used by Chandragupta I was:
questions.solution:
Imperial Titles of the Guptas

Chandragupta I was the first Gupta ruler to adopt the title Maharajadhiraja (King of Kings). This distinguished him from his predecessors, who held the simpler title of 'Maharaja', and signaled the rise of the Gupta dynasty as a major imperial power.

questions.question: ‘Pradeshtris’ word is used for which officers during the Mauryan Empire?
questions.option_4: Officers responsible for suppression of criminals
questions.solution:
Mauryan Administration: Pradeshtris

In the Mauryan administration, Pradeshtris were high-ranking officials responsible for the suppression of criminals and maintaining law and order. They also acted as circuit judges and performed administrative oversight of the districts.

questions.question: Who were the ‘Rajukas’ during the Mauryan period?
questions.solution:
The Role of Rajukas

Rajukas were provincial officials during the Mauryan period. Originally responsible for surveying land and collecting taxes, Ashoka later enhanced their powers, giving them judicial authority to ensure justice was served fairly in rural areas.

questions.question: Which metal was extensively used for coinage in the Mauryan Empire?
questions.solution:
Mauryan Coinage

Silver was the primary metal used for coinage in the Mauryan Empire. The most common coin was the Pana (a silver punch-marked coin), which typically featured symbols like the sun, moon, and various animals.

questions.question: Who among the following was NOT a Mauryan ruler?
questions.solution:
Pushyamitra Shunga

Pushyamitra was NOT a Mauryan ruler; he was the general who assassinated the last Mauryan king, Brihadratha, and established the Shunga Dynasty in 185 BCE. The other options (Devavarman, Samprati, and Brihadratha) were all successors of Ashoka.

questions.question: The Battle of Kalinga was fought in which year after Ashoka embraced Buddhism?
questions.solution:
The Battle of Kalinga

The Battle of Kalinga took place in 261 BCE, in the eighth year of Ashoka's coronation. The immense death and destruction caused by the war led Ashoka to renounce violence and embrace the path of peace and Buddhism.

questions.question: After which battle did Ashoka embrace Buddhism?
questions.solution:
Impact of Kalinga

Ashoka embraced Buddhism after witnessing the bloodshed of the Battle of Kalinga. This transformation is recorded in Major Rock Edict XIII, where he expresses deep remorse and declares that 'victory through Dhamma' is the only true victory.

questions.question: Which Greek ambassador visited the court of Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.solution:
Megasthenes

Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador sent by Seleucus I Nicator to the court of Chandragupta Maurya. He wrote the book Indica, which provides a detailed (though sometimes exaggerated) account of Mauryan administration and society.

questions.question: What was Ashoka's relation to Bindusara?
questions.solution:
Ashoka and Bindusara

Ashoka was the son of the second Mauryan Emperor, Bindusara. He served as the governor of Ujjain and Taxila before ascending the throne after a struggle for succession following his father's death.

questions.question: What does 'Dhamma' primarily refer to in Ashoka’s inscriptions?
questions.solution:
Concept of Dhamma

In Ashoka's inscriptions, Dhamma does not refer to a specific religion but to a moral law or code of conduct. It emphasized universal values like non-violence, respect for elders, kindness to animals, and religious tolerance.

questions.question: Ashoka appointed special officers to spread Dhamma, known as:
questions.solution:
Dhamma Mahamatras

To implement his social and moral reforms, Ashoka created a new class of officers called Dhamma Mahamatras. Their duty was to propagate the principles of Dhamma among people of all sects and oversee the welfare of the public.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding the materials of construction used for Ashokan pillars?
questions.option_1: They were largely made of stone.
questions.option_2: They were largely made of wood.
questions.option_3: They were largely made of concrete.
questions.option_4: They were largely made of iron.
questions.solution:
Ashokan Pillar Construction

The famous Ashokan pillars were almost exclusively made of stone, specifically Chunar sandstone. Each pillar was a monolith, meaning it was carved from a single massive block of stone, showcasing exceptional engineering and polishing skills.

questions.question: With whose assistance did Chandragupta Maurya overthrow the Nanda dynasty?
questions.solution:
Chanakya and the Nandas

Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty with the strategic assistance of Chanakya (also known as Kautilya or Vishnugupta). Chanakya sought to replace the tyrannical Nanda rule and mentored Chandragupta to become a great emperor.

questions.question: Who succeeded Ashoka to the throne?
questions.solution:
Succession of Ashoka

After the death of Ashoka, the Mauryan Empire began to decline. He was succeeded by his grandson Dasharatha in the eastern part of the empire, while another grandson, Samprati, reportedly ruled the western portions.

questions.question: Chandragupta was succeeded by which of the following rulers, who ruled between 297 and 273 BCE?
questions.solution:
Bindusara's Reign

Chandragupta Maurya was succeeded by his son Bindusara, who ruled from approximately 297 BCE to 273 BCE. Known as Amitraghata (Slayer of Foes), he maintained the vast empire inherited from his father and kept friendly ties with Greek kings.
